pull_requests.title: Document model validator execution order under inheritance
pull_requests.author: Nish2005karsh

reviews.body: So glad to see this included! I’ve seen so many people get confused by how validators run when inheritance is involved. This is going to be a lifesaver for anyone transitioning to v2.

This clarifies a non-obvious inheritance rule: subclass fter validators do not run inside a base wrap handler's inner handler(data) call. The calls trace example is minimal and makes execution order concrete without over-explaining decorator mechanics.

review_comments.body: To avoid any ambiguity where "once" might be misread as "only one time", it would be slightly clearer to use "after" or "as soon as":
review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -551,6 +551,42 @@ decorator.
 
     Overriding a model validator in a subclass will override the base class' validator, and thus only the subclass' version of said validator will be called.
 
+    Model validators are ordered by their mode rather than by the class they are defined in. In particular, a *wrap* validator
+    defined in a base class does **not** wrap the *after* validators defined in a subclass: *after* validators always run once

review_comments.body: Thanks @strangeleo02! Updated "once" → "after" to remove the ambiguity.
review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -551,6 +551,42 @@ decorator.
 
     Overriding a model validator in a subclass will override the base class' validator, and thus only the subclass' version of said validator will be called.
 
+    Model validators are ordered by their mode rather than by the class they are defined in. In particular, a *wrap* validator
+    defined in a base class does **not** wrap the *after* validators defined in a subclass: *after* validators always run once

pull_requests.title: Make JSON validation by iteration consistent with getitem approach
pull_requests.author: davidhewitt

reviews.body: Took a closer look at the new `validate_json_by_iteration` extra-key handling — nice cleanup overall. Left two notes inline: one on the `Vec`-based `possible_extra` becoming quadratic on the many-unknown-keys shape (with a suggested `IndexMap` + consumed-keys-set approach to get back to O(N)), and a non-blocking question about whether the per-field `retain` is load-bearing for correctness. Neither is a blocker — mostly want to flag the worst-case path since the CodSpeed suite doesn't seem to cover it.

Switching JSON object validation to indexmap iteration for consistent ordering with getitem is a subtle correctness fix — field validation order can affect error reporting and partial validation behavior. Adding indexmap as an explicit dependency makes the ordering guarantee auditable in Cargo.lock.

review_comments.body:
performance: `possible_extra` is a `Vec`, which makes the extra-key bookkeeping quadratic on the "many unknown keys" shape.

Two scans compound:
- L483 `possible_extra.iter_mut().find(|(k, _)| *k == key)` is an O(E) linear scan per buffered key, so building `possible_extra` is O(E²).
- L504 `possible_extra.retain(|(k, _)| *k != lookup_path.first_key())` is an O(E) scan per field, so the field loop adds O(F·E).

Total is O(E² + F·E). For `extra='allow'`/`'forbid'` inputs that are mostly unknown keys (E ≈ N), that collapses to O(N²). The previous getitem path buffered extras straight into a `PyDict` via `set_item` — O(1) amortized per key, O(N) overall — so this is a worst-case regression on that specific shape. The CodSpeed run here (+7.27%, 8 benchmarks) doesn't include a large-extra-dict case, so the regression isn't visible in CI.

Suggested approach: back `possible_extra` with an insertion-ordered map (e.g. `indexmap::IndexMap<&str, &JsonValue>`) so the L483 dedup becomes an O(1) `insert` while still preserving appearance order. For L504, rather than an O(E) `retain` per field, collect consumed first-keys into an `AHashSet<&str>` during the field loop (this file already uses `AHashSet<&str>` for `used_keys` on the dict path) and skip them in the final emit loop at L514 — that makes the field-side cost O(F + E). Net result is back to O(N).

question (non-blocking): is the per-field `retain` at L504 load-bearing for correctness, or purely an optimization to avoid emitting a key that a later field consumes? If a buffered key can only ever be claimed by exactly one field's `first_key`, a single membership check against a consumed-keys set at emit time would be equivalent and avoids the repeated removal. Want to confirm I'm not missing a case where the same buffered key must survive for one field but be dropped for another.

pull_requests.title: Validate falsy `AliasGenerator` outputs
pull_requests.author: jiyujie2006

reviews.body:
## Review: Validate falsy AliasGenerator outputs

This is a clean, well-targeted fix. Here are my observations:

### What's Good

- **Correct root cause identification.** The bug was a truthiness check (`if alias`) that silently dropped valid falsy alias values (like `""`) and let invalid falsy values (like `0`, `False`, `[]`) bypass the type-check, reaching pydantic-core and producing confusing `SchemaError`s instead of the intended `TypeError`.
- **Minimal, focused change.** Only two lines in production code are modified — both truthiness guards replaced with explicit `is not None` checks. This is exactly the right fix.
- **Good test expansion.** Parameterizing `test_alias_generator_with_invalid_callables` with `[1, 0, False, []]` covers both the previously-tested truthy case and the newly-fixed falsy cases. This ensures the fix is correct and prevents regressions.

### Subtle Behavioral Improvement Worth Noting

The old code had a **second hidden bug**: if an alias generator returned `""` (empty string), it was silently treated as "no alias" because `if alias` evaluated to `False` for `""`. The new `is not None` check now correctly distinguishes between `None` (alias intentionally unset) and `""` (a valid `str` alias, even if empty). This is a subtle but meaningful correctness improvement beyond just catching falsy invalid types.

### Potential Concerns

1. **The test closure issue.** The parametrized test uses `lambda x: return_value` — `return_value` is captured from the closure's scope (the parametrize fixture), not as a default argument. This works correctly here because `pytest.mark.parametrize` creates a new scope for each parameter, but it's a pattern that can bite. Not blocking, just something to be aware of.

2. **`None` return semantics.** The PR preserves the existing behavior where returning `None` from an alias generator means "no alias". This is reasonable and documented, but it might be worth adding a brief docstring note that `None` is the only "skip alias" sentinel — `""` is now treated as a real alias. Again, not blocking.

### Summary

The PR is well-written, correctly fixes a real bug (both the reported falsy-type bypass and the implicit `""`-dropping), includes appropriate tests, and keeps the diff minimal. I'd be happy to see this merged. LGTM 👍

+---
+name: pydantic
+description: Pydantic is a Python data validation and serialization library, based on type hints. Use this skill whenever you need to do relatively complex data modeling using Pydantic, e.g. when adding constraints, defining a model hierarchy with subclasses, etc.
+---
+
+# Pydantic Validation
+
+In a nutshell, Pydantic is dataclasses with runtime validation. It leverages type hints
+to understand how validation (and serialization) should be performed. It is mostly useful
+when dealing with external untrusted data, for example when defining an HTTP API.
+
+You shouldn't use Pydantic to define classes that are instantiated within the user code.
+It is usually better to use vanilla classes (or standard library dataclasses) in this case,
+as a static type checker will already catch type mismatches.

- `pydantic-core-x.y.z-cp314-cp314-pyemscripten_2026_0_wasm32.whl`
- `pydantic-core-x.y.z-cp313-cp313-pyemscripten_2025_0_wasm32.whl`
- `pydantic-core-x.y.z-cp312-cp312-pyemscripten_2024_0_wasm32.whl`

?

PEP 783 says that the `pyemscripten_*_wasm32` platform definition is not conceptually about Python, which looks true.

But Pyodide doesn't support arbitrary combinations of CPython ABI and `pyemscripten_*` platform.

| Pyodide Version | CPython Version | PEP 783 Support | Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0.22.1 | 3.10 | `pyemscripten_*` not supported | ❌ |
| 0.25.1 | 3.11 | `pyemscripten_*` not supported | ❌ |
| 0.27.7 | 3.12 | `pyemscripten_*` not supported | ❌ |
| 0.29.4 | 3.13 | `pyemscripten_2025_0_wasm32` | ✅ works |
| 314.0.0-alpha.2 | 3.14 | `pyemscripten_2026_0_wasm32` | ✅ works |

So if we are targeting Pyodide with our pyemscripten wheels, the full wheel suite would be:

- `pydantic_core-*-cp313-cp313-pyemscripten_2025_0_wasm32.whl`
- `pydantic_core-*-cp314-cp314-pyemscripten_2026_0_wasm32.whl`

Thanks, handled in https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/13199. I'm releasing 2.14.0a1 soon, so you can test out the wheel (https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/13186#pullrequestreview-4297556338 still applies, might be worth waiting for a final Pyodide release before using in production).

pull_requests.title: Accept scientific notation in Decimal JSON schema serialization pattern
pull_requests.author: jbbqqf

reviews.body: Took a look at the scientific-notation fix — confirmed it round-trips correctly (no valid `str(Decimal)` output gets rejected), so the core goal works. Left one inline question about the constraint semantics: once the exponent suffix is allowed, `max_digits`/`decimal_places` no longer bound the actual value for inputs carrying an exponent. Not a blocker — more a question about whether the emitted schema should still advertise those limits, or note the relaxation. The inline comment already flags that bounding sci-notation is intractable, which I agree with; this is just the narrower documentation/intent angle.

review_comments.body: question: once `exp_suffix` (L709) is appended, the emitted schema's `max_digits` / `decimal_places` constraints stop bounding the actual value for any input carrying an exponent. The coefficient is length-limited by the `[\d.]{1,max_digits+1}` lookaheads (L725/L736), and trailing zeros get absorbed by the following `0*`, but the exponent `\d+` is unbounded — so e.g. for `max_digits=8, decimal_places=4`, both `1.5E+10` (11 integer digits) and `1.5E-10` (effectively 11 decimal places) validate. The inline comment already notes that bounding scientific notation by digit counts is intractable, which I agree with; my question is narrower: should the schema still *advertise* `max_digits`/`decimal_places` when a subset of accepted inputs provably violate them, and is there a difference in intent between the validation-mode and serialization-mode schemas here? A short note in the schema description (or scoping the relaxation to serialization mode only) might prevent a consumer from assuming the emitted `pattern` enforces the digit limits it names.
review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -697,9 +697,23 @@ def get_decimal_pattern(schema: core_schema.DecimalSchema) -> str:
             max_digits = schema.get('max_digits')
             decimal_places = schema.get('decimal_places')
 
-            pattern = (
-                r'^(?!^[-+.]*$)[+-]?0*'  # check it is not empty string and not one or sequence of ".+-" characters.
-            )
+            # pydantic-core serializes `Decimal` via `str(d)`, which yields
+            # scientific notation for values whose canonical exponent makes
+            # fixed-point output verbose (e.g. `Decimal('0.0000001') -> '1E-7'`).
+            # Allow an optional `[eE][+-]?\d+` suffix so the schema accepts
+            # everything `model_dump_json` itself can emit. Digit-count limits
+            # are enforced on the coefficient form only; bounding scientific
+            # notation by digit counts via regex is intractable, and rejecting
+            # valid serializer output is the worse failure mode (it breaks
+            # round-tripping the JSON through the schema). See issue #13089.
+            exp_suffix = r'(?:[eE][+-]?\d+)?'

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+# Invoked by .github/workflows/ci.yml. Requires `pyodide` on PATH and the
+# freshly built wheels in pydantic-core/dist/ (pyemscripten) and dist/
+# (pure-Python pydantic).
+set -euo pipefail
+
+VENV_DIR=".venv-pyodide"
+
+if [ ! -d "${VENV_DIR}" ]; then
+    pyodide venv "${VENV_DIR}"
+fi
+# shellcheck disable=SC1090
+source "${VENV_DIR}/bin/activate"
+
+shopt -s nullglob
+# Accept the PEP 783 `pyemscripten_<abi>_wasm32` tag and the legacy
+# `emscripten_X_Y_Z_wasm32` tag so toolchain regressions surface here.
+core_wheels=(
+    pydantic-core/dist/pydantic_core-*-cp*-pyemscripten_*_wasm32.whl
+    pydantic-core/dist/pydantic_core-*-cp*-emscripten_*_wasm32.whl
+)
+pyd_wheels=(dist/pydantic-*-py3-none-any.whl)
+shopt -u nullglob
+
+if [ ${#core_wheels[@]} -eq 0 ]; then
+    echo "no pydantic-core wasm wheel in pydantic-core/dist/" >&2
+    exit 1
+fi
+if [ ${#pyd_wheels[@]} -eq 0 ]; then
+    echo "no pydantic wheel in dist/" >&2
+    exit 1
+fi
+
+# --force-reinstall here so iteration picks up freshly-built binaries even
+# when the wheel's version string is unchanged.
+pip install --force-reinstall --no-deps "${core_wheels[0]}" "${pyd_wheels[0]}"
+# Deliberately omitted (no wasm wheels / unsupported in Pyodide):
+#   pytest-timeout (uses signal.setitimer; `timeout` marker registered in
+#       pyproject.toml as a no-op), pytest-examples (depends on black/aiohttp),
+#   pytest-benchmark, pytest-codspeed, pytest-memray, pytest-run-parallel,
+#   cloudpickle, cffi, pandas, numpy.
+pip install \
+    pytest \
+    pytest-mock \
+    pytest-pretty \
+    dirty-equals \
+    hypothesis \
+    inline-snapshot \
+    typing-inspection \
+    typing-extensions \
+    annotated-types \
+    eval-type-backport \
+    jsonschema \
+    pytz \
+    tzdata
+
+# `--override-ini=addopts=` strips the project's `--benchmark-*` defaults
+# (pytest-benchmark has no wasm wheel; pytest would reject them as
+# unrecognized). `-p no:timeout` because pytest-timeout is not installed.
+pytest_log=$(mktemp)
+plain_log=$(mktemp)
+trap 'rm -f "${pytest_log}" "${plain_log}"' EXIT
+set +e
+pytest \
+    --override-ini='addopts=' \
+    -p no:cacheprovider \
+    -p no:timeout \
+    --ignore=tests/pydantic_core/benchmarks \
+    --ignore=tests/mypy \
+    --ignore=tests/typechecking \
+    --ignore=tests/benchmarks \
+    --ignore=tests/test_docs.py \
+    tests/pydantic_core \
+    tests 2>&1 | tee "${pytest_log}"
+pytest_exit=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
+set -e
+
+# Strip ANSI escapes pytest-pretty emits, so the guard below greps the same
+# text humans see rather than the colourised byte stream.
+sed -E $'s/\x1b\\[[0-9;]*[a-zA-Z]//g' "${pytest_log}" > "${plain_log}"
+
+# Pyodide 314 alphas crash during CPython interpreter teardown after pytest
+# returns -- the wasm vtable goes south *after* the test summary prints,
+# making the process exit non-zero on x86_64 GH runners. Trust pytest's own
+# summary line: if it reports a clean run and the only failure signal is the
+# teardown crash, treat the job as green.

+# --force-reinstall here so iteration picks up freshly-built binaries even
+# when the wheel's version string is unchanged.
+pip install --force-reinstall --no-deps "${core_wheels[0]}" "${pyd_wheels[0]}"
+# Deliberately omitted (no wasm wheels / unsupported in Pyodide):
+#   pytest-timeout (uses signal.setitimer; `timeout` marker registered in
+#       pyproject.toml as a no-op), pytest-examples (depends on black/aiohttp),
+#   pytest-benchmark, pytest-codspeed, pytest-memray, pytest-run-parallel,
+#   cloudpickle, cffi, pandas, numpy.
+pip install \
+    pytest \
+    pytest-mock \
+    pytest-pretty \
+    dirty-equals \
+    hypothesis \
+    inline-snapshot \
+    typing-inspection \
+    typing-extensions \
+    annotated-types \
+    eval-type-backport \
+    jsonschema \
+    pytz \
+    tzdata
+
+# `--override-ini=addopts=` strips the project's `--benchmark-*` defaults
+# (pytest-benchmark has no wasm wheel; pytest would reject them as
+# unrecognized). `-p no:timeout` because pytest-timeout is not installed.
+pytest_log=$(mktemp)
+plain_log=$(mktemp)
+trap 'rm -f "${pytest_log}" "${plain_log}"' EXIT
+set +e
+pytest \
+    --override-ini='addopts=' \
+    -p no:cacheprovider \
+    -p no:timeout \
+    --ignore=tests/pydantic_core/benchmarks \
+    --ignore=tests/mypy \
+    --ignore=tests/typechecking \
+    --ignore=tests/benchmarks \
+    --ignore=tests/test_docs.py \
+    tests/pydantic_core \
+    tests 2>&1 | tee "${pytest_log}"
+pytest_exit=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
+set -e
+
+# Strip ANSI escapes pytest-pretty emits, so the guard below greps the same
+# text humans see rather than the colourised byte stream.
+sed -E $'s/\x1b\\[[0-9;]*[a-zA-Z]//g' "${pytest_log}" > "${plain_log}"
+
+# Pyodide 314 alphas crash during CPython interpreter teardown after pytest
+# returns -- the wasm vtable goes south *after* the test summary prints,
+# making the process exit non-zero on x86_64 GH runners. Trust pytest's own
+# summary line: if it reports a clean run and the only failure signal is the
+# teardown crash, treat the job as green.

pull_requests.title: Adapt `pydantic-core` linker flags on macOS
pull_requests.author: Viicos

issue_comments.body:
Hi @Viicos,

This was discussed in https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/issues/7847 (2022). At the time, @reaperhulk said:

"I don't have any issue with adding that flag to our wheel builders going forward."

The regression went away on its own then (a rustc bump changed the default padding).

In the issue you opened, you mentioned:

> Homebrew's post-install fixup (install_name_tool) needs that padding to rewrite dylib paths after installation. When it's absent, brew install/brew upgrade fails for any formula that vendors pydantic-core.

> In the issue you opened, you mentioned:
> 
> > Homebrew's post-install fixup (install_name_tool) needs that padding to rewrite dylib paths after installation. When it's absent, brew install/brew upgrade fails for any formula that vendors pydantic-core.

@Viicos, there's no Homebrew issue that I'm aware of. It was first caught by a user running brew upgrade on an internal formula that vendors cryptography. The error surfaced when cryptography 46.0.4 shipped, which was the first release built on macos-15 runners.

The closest public tracking is:
- The pyca/cryptography PR that just merged: https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/pull/14777
- A similar fix in an unrelated project that hit the same thing: https://github.com/dkdc-io/zorto/pull/155

The root cause is documented in the cryptography PR description if you want to link to it from here.
Thanks @washingtoneg, @davidhewitt I'm merging but feel free to investigate deeper if this needs to live in PyO3.
